response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
  "opinion": "I've been a mechanic for over forty years, so I'm used to fixing things that don't work right. Social media is kind of like that—it's got all sorts of parts, some genuine and useful, others full of glitches and nonsense. As someone with my own creative side, I can see how fake content messes up the whole system, just like a bad spark plug would ruin an engine. My dad has a PhD in engineering—he could probably tell you about counterfeit parts from his work—but he's not around anymore; that's why I spend only one hour a day on social media.

The way I deal with it is simple: if something doesn't look right, like someone posting fake reviews or misleading info about car repairs, I ignore it and move on. My own engine shop in Los Angeles gets tons of fake messages claiming to be from customers who never actually called us. Sometimes they're funny—the kind of humor that comes from creativity—but most times they're just plain irritating.

I believe the best way is education mixed with common sense. Just like I teach my apprentices how to spot a fake part by its build and material, people need to learn what makes real information hold up online. My kids help me stay updated—they're always on their phones—but I still stick to one hour daily.

Another thing: don't believe everything you see. Social media feeds can be like looking under the hood—if there's smoke coming out of something, it might not be running smooth.",

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
I've been a mechanic for over forty years, so I'm used to fixing things that don't work right. Social media is kind of like that—it's got all sorts of parts, some genuine and useful, others full of glitches and nonsense. As someone with my own creative side, I can see how fake content messes up the whole system, just like a bad spark plug would ruin an engine. My dad has a PhD in engineering—he could probably tell you about counterfeit parts from his work—but he's not around anymore; that's why I spend only one hour a day on social media.

The way I deal with it is simple: if something doesn't look right, like someone posting fake reviews or misleading info about car repairs, I ignore it and move on. My own engine shop in Los Angeles gets tons of fake messages claiming to be from customers who never actually called us. Sometimes they're funny—the kind of humor that comes from creativity—but most times they're just plain irritating.

I believe the best way is education mixed with common sense. Just like I teach my apprentices how to spot a fake part by its build and material, people need to learn what makes real information hold up online. My kids help me stay updated—they're always on their phones—but I still stick to one hour daily.

Another thing: don't believe everything you see. Social media feeds can be like looking under the hood—if there's smoke coming out of something, it might not be running smooth.
